title: Module 3 quiz — Technical SEO That Actually Matters

questions.prompt: Google keeps rewriting your <title> tag in the SERP. Which is the most likely cause?

choices.text: Random Google bug — file a support ticket.

Your title misrepresents the page (keyword stuffing, all-caps, clickbait, or significantly off-topic). Google rewrites when your title doesn't match the content. The fix is to write an accurate, useful title.
You have multiple <h1> tags on the page.
Your page is too long.

questions.explanation: Google's title rewrites are designed to surface accurate, helpful titles to users. Common triggers: keyword stuffing, all-caps, exaggerated/clickbait language, or significant mismatch between title and content. The fix is to write a genuine, descriptive title — not to fight the rewrite.

questions.prompt: You're migrating your site from example.com to newexample.com tomorrow. Which single thing matters most for protecting organic traffic?

choices.text: Update your meta keywords on every page.
Set up 301 (permanent) redirects from every old URL to its corresponding new URL — and avoid redirect chains.
Submit a new sitemap.
Add the new domain to Google Search Console.

questions.explanation: 301 redirects are the single most important step in any URL migration — they tell Google the move is permanent and pass ranking signal from old URLs to new ones. Without them, you lose almost all inherited authority. Submitting a sitemap and verifying in Search Console are helpful follow-ups but secondary.

questions.prompt: An SEO consultant claims you have a 'duplicate content penalty' because your URLs are reachable at both http:// and https://. What's the most accurate assessment?

choices.text: They're right — that's a major penalty.
There is no duplicate content penalty. What you have is canonical ambiguity. Fix it by 301-redirecting http to https, then consolidating to one canonical (with or without www).
Block one of the protocols in robots.txt.
Submit a reconsideration request.

questions.explanation: Google has stated for years: 'Having duplicate content on your site is not a violation of our spam policies.' What duplicates create is canonical confusion and wasted crawl budget. The fix for HTTP/HTTPS is 301 redirects to the secure version — not a penalty appeal, not a robots.txt block.

questions.prompt: Which is the best alt text for a recipe photo of a finished sourdough loaf cross-sectioned to show the crumb?

choices.text: alt="IMG_4729.jpg"
alt="Sourdough loaf cross-section showing open crumb with large irregular holes"
alt="image of sourdough bread sourdough loaf bread baking sourdough recipe best sourdough"
Empty alt: alt=""

questions.explanation: Good alt text describes what the image actually shows, in context, concisely. (a) is the filename, useless. (c) is keyword-stuffed — doesn't help SEO, hurts accessibility. (d) is for purely decorative images, not recipe content where the image carries real information about what the dish should look like.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a real, documented Google ranking signal?

choices.text: Page <meta keywords> tag content.
Exact word count of articles.
Strict <h1>/<h2>/<h3> hierarchical order.
Page experience signals (Core Web Vitals: loading, interactivity, visual stability).

questions.explanation: Core Web Vitals are part of Google's page experience signals and do affect rankings (especially as a tiebreaker between similar-quality pages). The other three are myths: meta keywords is ignored, there's no magical word count, and heading order matters for accessibility but not for SEO ranking.
